topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI is a modern, high-performance web framework for building APIs with Python 3.8+ based on standard Python type hints. It is one of the fastest Python frameworks available, on par with NodeJS and Go, and automatically generates interactive API documentation (Swagger UI and ReDoc) from your code. FastAPI leverages Pydantic for data validation and serialization, and Starlette for the web parts, making it production-ready out of the box.
warnings: Requires Python 3.8 or higher
Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies
FastAPI does not include a database layer — you must integrate one separately

codeBlocks.label: Minimal App
Run Server
codeBlocks.code: uvicorn main:app --reload

apiReference.name: FastAPI()
apiReference.signature: FastAPI(title, description, version, docs_url, redoc_url, openapi_url, debug)
apiReference.description: Main application class. Creates an ASGI application instance that handles routing, middleware, and dependency injection.

params.name: title
params.desc: API title shown in the auto-generated docs

params.name: description
params.desc: Markdown description shown in the docs

params.name: version
params.desc: API version string

params.name: docs_url
params.desc: URL for Swagger UI. Set to None to disable.

params.name: redoc_url
params.desc: URL for ReDoc. Set to None to disable.

params.name: debug
params.desc: Enable debug mode for detailed error responses

topics.title: Installation
topics.description: Installing FastAPI requires Python 3.8+. The recommended approach is to use a virtual environment and install both fastapi and uvicorn (the ASGI server). You can optionally install the 'standard' extras bundle which includes additional dependencies like email validation and pydantic extras.
warnings: Always activate your virtual environment before installing packages
Use 'fastapi[standard]' to get all recommended dependencies including uvicorn

codeBlocks.label: Create venv
codeBlocks.code:
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

codeBlocks.label: Install FastAPI
codeBlocks.code: pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard]

codeBlocks.label: Install with all extras
codeBlocks.code: pip install "fastapi[standard]"

codeBlocks.label: Verify installation
codeBlocks.code: python -c "import fastapi; print(fastapi.__version__)"

topics.title: First App
topics.description: Creating your first FastAPI application involves three steps: importing FastAPI, creating an app instance, and defining route functions using decorator syntax. FastAPI uses Python type annotations to validate inputs and generate documentation automatically. The function return value is automatically serialized to JSON.
warnings: Your main file must be named consistently with what you pass to uvicorn (e.g., main:app)
Response models are inferred from return type hints when possible

codeBlocks.label: main.py

topics.title: Running the Server
topics.description: FastAPI applications are served using an ASGI server. Uvicorn is the most popular choice and is included with the standard install. The --reload flag enables hot-reloading during development. For production, remove --reload and consider using Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers for process management.
warnings: Never use --reload in production environments
Use Gunicorn with uvicorn workers for multi-process production deployments

codeBlocks.label: Development
codeBlocks.code: uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

codeBlocks.label: Production (Gunicorn)
codeBlocks.code: gunicorn main:app -w 4 -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker --bind 0.0.0.0:8000

codeBlocks.label: Custom host/port
codeBlocks.code: uvicorn main:app --reload --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3000

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI routing maps HTTP methods and URL paths to Python functions called path operation functions. Routes are defined using decorators on the app instance (or an APIRouter for modular apps). FastAPI automatically parses path parameters, query parameters, and request bodies based on function signatures and type hints.
warnings: Route order matters — more specific routes must be defined before generic ones
Use APIRouter to split large applications into multiple files

codeBlocks.label: Basic Routes

topics.title: Path Operations
topics.description: Path operation decorators bind a URL path and HTTP method to a function. Each decorator accepts optional parameters for metadata like tags, summary, description, response model, and status code. These appear in the auto-generated OpenAPI documentation. You can also use deprecated=True to mark operations without removing them.
warnings: The @app.get() decorator creates a GET endpoint — do not confuse it with Python's built-in get()
Setting response_model overrides the return type hint for serialization

codeBlocks.label: Decorator Options
APIRouter

apiReference.name: @app.get()
apiReference.signature: @app.get(path, response_model, status_code, tags, summary, description, deprecated)
apiReference.description: Registers a GET path operation. Same options apply to @app.post(), @app.put(), @app.delete(), @app.patch().

params.name: path
params.desc: URL path, may include path parameters like {item_id}

params.name: response_model
params.desc: Pydantic model used to filter and validate the response

params.name: status_code
params.desc: Default HTTP status code for successful responses

params.name: tags
params.desc: Tags for grouping in OpenAPI docs

params.name: deprecated
params.desc: Mark the operation as deprecated in docs

topics.title: Path Parameters
topics.description: Path parameters are dynamic segments of a URL defined using curly braces in the path string. FastAPI automatically extracts them from the URL and passes them as function arguments. Type annotations on the parameter drive automatic validation — if a non-integer is passed where int is expected, FastAPI returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity error.
warnings: Fixed paths like /users/me must be declared BEFORE parameterized paths like /users/{user_id}
Use Enum subclasses to restrict path parameter values to a known set

codeBlocks.label: Basic path parameter
Enum restriction
Path parameter with validation

apiReference.name: Path()
apiReference.signature: Path(default, title, description, gt, ge, lt, le, min_length, max_length, regex)
apiReference.description: Declares a path parameter with validation constraints and metadata.

params.name: gt
params.desc: Value must be greater than this

params.name: ge
params.desc: Value must be greater than or equal to this

params.name: lt
params.desc: Value must be less than this

params.name: le
params.desc: Value must be less than or equal to this

params.name: description
params.desc: Human-readable description shown in docs

topics.title: Query Parameters
topics.description: Query parameters are key-value pairs in the URL after the ? symbol (e.g. /items?skip=0&limit=10). In FastAPI, any function parameter that is not a path parameter and not a Pydantic model is treated as a query parameter. They can be optional by providing a default value, or required by using no default.
warnings: Query parameters are strings in the raw URL — FastAPI automatically converts them to the annotated type
Use Query() to add validation constraints or mark a query param as required with no default

codeBlocks.label: Optional query params
Required query param
Optional with None

apiReference.name: Query()
apiReference.signature: Query(default, title, description, min_length, max_length, regex, gt, ge, lt, le, alias)
apiReference.description: Declares a query parameter with validation and metadata.

params.name: default
params.desc: Default value. Use ... (Ellipsis) to make required.

params.name: min_length
params.desc: Minimum string length

params.name: max_length
params.desc: Maximum string length

params.name: alias
params.desc: Alternative name used in the URL

topics.title: HTTP Methods
topics.description: FastAPI supports all standard HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, OPTIONS, HEAD, and TRACE. Each maps semantically to a CRUD operation. GET retrieves data, POST creates resources, PUT replaces resources, PATCH partially updates them, and DELETE removes them. Using the correct method is important for REST conventions and caching behavior.
warnings: GET requests must not have a request body — use query or path parameters instead
PUT should replace the entire resource, PATCH should update only the provided fields

codeBlocks.label: All HTTP methods

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI provides rich tools for handling incoming request data and shaping outgoing responses. Request bodies are parsed from JSON using Pydantic models. Response models filter and validate output. Status codes communicate the result of an operation. Custom headers can be added via the Response parameter or Response class.
warnings: Always define Pydantic models for request bodies — never parse raw JSON manually
Use response_model to prevent accidentally leaking sensitive fields like passwords

codeBlocks.label: Request and response models
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class UserIn(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: str
    email: str

class UserOut(BaseModel):
    username: str
    email: str

@app.post("/users", response_model=UserOut)
def create_user(user: UserIn):
    # password is never included in response
    return user

topics.title: Request Body
topics.description: A request body is data sent from the client to the server in the HTTP request body, typically as JSON. In FastAPI, you declare a request body by creating a Pydantic BaseModel subclass and using it as a function parameter type. FastAPI automatically reads the JSON body, validates it against the model, and passes the parsed object to your function.
warnings: Request bodies are only valid for POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE — not GET
Nest models or use List[Model] for complex structures

codeBlocks.label: Basic request body
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    description: str | None = None
    price: float
    tax: float = 0.1

@app.post("/items")
def create_item(item: Item):
    item_dict = item.model_dump()
    item_dict["total"] = item.price + (item.price * item.tax)
    return item_dict

codeBlocks.label: Nested models

apiReference.name: BaseModel
apiReference.description: Pydantic's base class for defining request/response schemas with automatic validation.

params.name: field
params.desc: Declare each field as a class attribute with type annotation

topics.title: Response Model
topics.description: The response_model parameter on path operation decorators specifies a Pydantic model that FastAPI uses to filter, validate, and serialize the response. Any fields in the returned data that are not in the response_model are automatically excluded. This is the primary mechanism for preventing sensitive data leakage.
warnings: If you return a dict with extra keys, response_model will filter them out silently
Use response_model_exclude_unset=True to omit fields with default values

codeBlocks.label: response_model usage
response_model_exclude_unset

topics.title: Status Codes
topics.description: HTTP status codes communicate the result of a request. FastAPI defaults to 200 OK for most routes. You can override the default via the status_code parameter on the decorator. The fastapi.status module provides named constants for all standard codes. For dynamic status codes based on logic, use the Response parameter.
warnings: Use 201 Created for POST endpoints that create a resource
Return 204 No Content for DELETE operations with no response body

codeBlocks.label: Setting status codes
HTTPException

apiReference.name: HTTPException
apiReference.description: Raise this exception to return an HTTP error response from any path operation.

params.name: status_code
params.desc: HTTP status code (e.g. 404, 422, 500)

params.name: detail
params.desc: Error detail, serialized to JSON in the response body

params.name: headers
params.desc: Optional headers to include in the error response

topics.title: Headers
topics.description: FastAPI allows you to read incoming request headers as function parameters using the Header dependency, and to set custom response headers by injecting the Response object or returning a Response directly. Header parameter names use underscore instead of hyphen (FastAPI converts automatically). Common use cases include authentication tokens, caching, and content negotiation.
warnings: HTTP headers are case-insensitive — FastAPI handles this automatically
Header names with hyphens must be declared with underscores in Python (e.g. x_token maps to X-Token)

codeBlocks.label: Reading request headers
Setting response headers

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI's dependency injection system (Depends) allows you to declare shared logic — such as database connections, authentication checks, and query parameter parsing — in reusable functions. Dependencies are declared as function parameters using Depends(). FastAPI resolves the dependency graph automatically, handles caching within a request, and supports async dependencies.
warnings: Dependencies are re-used within a request by default (cached). Use use_cache=False to disable this.
Dependencies can themselves have dependencies — FastAPI resolves the full graph

codeBlocks.label: Simple dependency

topics.title: Depends()
topics.description: Depends() is the core function for declaring dependencies in FastAPI. You pass a callable (function or class) to Depends(), and FastAPI calls it before your route handler runs, injecting the return value. This is ideal for shared query parameter parsing, authentication checks, and other cross-cutting concerns.
warnings: The callable passed to Depends() can be async — FastAPI handles both sync and async dependencies
Class-based dependencies are supported and useful for grouping related logic

codeBlocks.label: Function dependency
Class-based dependency

apiReference.name: Depends()
apiReference.description: Declares a dependency on another callable. FastAPI injects the return value of the dependency into the route function.

params.name: dependency
params.desc: The callable to use as a dependency. If None, uses the type annotation.

params.name: use_cache
params.desc: Whether to reuse the dependency result within the same request

apiReference.returns: The return value of the dependency callable

topics.title: Sub-dependencies
topics.description: Dependencies can themselves have dependencies, forming a dependency tree. FastAPI resolves the full graph automatically, ensuring each dependency is called in the correct order. Sub-dependencies receive the same caching behavior — if a dependency is used multiple times in the tree, it is only called once per request by default.
warnings: Circular dependencies will cause an error — ensure the dependency graph is a DAG (directed acyclic graph)

codeBlocks.label: Chained dependencies

topics.title: Scoped Dependencies
topics.description: Dependencies can be scoped to the entire application, a specific router, or individual path operations. Router-level dependencies apply to all routes in that router, making them ideal for authentication guards. Application-level dependencies apply globally. Use yield in a dependency to create a context manager pattern for resources like database sessions.
warnings: Dependencies with yield run code after the response is sent — ideal for cleanup (closing DB connections)
Exception handling in yield dependencies uses try/finally, not try/except

codeBlocks.label: Yield dependency (DB session)
Router-scoped dependency

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI does not include built-in database support, but integrates seamlessly with SQLModel (built on SQLAlchemy and Pydantic), raw SQLAlchemy, async databases, and many others. SQLModel is the recommended choice for FastAPI applications as it unifies Pydantic models and SQLAlchemy ORM models into a single class definition.
warnings: Always run database operations in a background thread or use async when using blocking ORMs
Use Alembic for production database migrations — never rely on create_all() in production

codeBlocks.label: SQLModel overview
codeBlocks.code:
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field, Session, create_engine, select

class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int | None = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)
    name: str
    secret_name: str
    age: int | None = None

engine = create_engine("sqlite:///database.db")
SQLModel.metadata.create_all(engine)

topics.title: SQLModel Setup
topics.description: SQLModel combines SQLAlchemy's ORM power with Pydantic's validation in a single class. Models with table=True become database tables; models without it are used as Pydantic schemas for request/response validation. This eliminates the need to maintain separate ORM models and API schemas.
warnings: Install SQLModel with: pip install sqlmodel
Models with table=True should not be used directly as response_model — create a separate schema model

codeBlocks.label: Install SQLModel
codeBlocks.code: pip install sqlmodel

codeBlocks.label: Define models and engine
codeBlocks.code:
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field, Session, create_engine, select
from typing import Optional

# Table model (maps to DB)
class HeroBase(SQLModel):
    name: str
    secret_name: str
    age: Optional[int] = None

class Hero(HeroBase, table=True):
    id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)

# API schema models
class HeroCreate(HeroBase):
    pass

class HeroRead(HeroBase):
    id: int

# Database engine
DATABASE_URL = "sqlite:///./heroes.db"
engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL, echo=True)

def create_db():
    SQLModel.metadata.create_all(engine)

topics.title: CRUD Operations
topics.description: CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four fundamental database operations. With SQLModel and FastAPI, each operation corresponds to a specific HTTP method and route. Sessions are injected via Depends() using a generator dependency, ensuring proper connection management.
warnings: Always commit and refresh after creating or updating a record
Use session.get() for lookups by primary key — it is more efficient than a full query

codeBlocks.label: Full CRUD example

topics.title: Async Sessions
topics.description: For high-concurrency applications, use async database sessions with SQLModel and asyncpg (for PostgreSQL) or aiosqlite (for SQLite). Async sessions use AsyncSession from sqlmodel and require async def path operations. This allows FastAPI to handle other requests while database I/O is awaited.
warnings: Install aiosqlite for async SQLite: pip install aiosqlite
All async DB operations must be awaited — forgetting await is a common source of bugs

codeBlocks.label: Async setup
codeBlocks.code:
from sqlmodel import SQLModel
from sqlmodel.ext.asyncio.session import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker

DATABASE_URL = "sqlite+aiosqlite:///./heroes.db"
async_engine = create_async_engine(DATABASE_URL, echo=True)

async def get_session():
    async with AsyncSession(async_engine) as session:
        yield session

codeBlocks.label: Async CRUD
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from sqlmodel import select
from sqlmodel.ext.asyncio.session import AsyncSession

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/heroes")
async def list_heroes(db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session)):
    result = await db.exec(select(Hero))
    return result.all()

@app.post("/heroes")
async def create_hero(hero: HeroCreate, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session)):
    db_hero = Hero.model_validate(hero)
    db.add(db_hero)
    await db.commit()
    await db.refresh(db_hero)
    return db_hero

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: FastAPI provides built-in support for several authentication schemes via its security utilities. The most common approach for modern APIs is OAuth2 with JWT (JSON Web Tokens). The flow is: user sends credentials → server validates → server returns a signed JWT token → client sends the token in the Authorization header for all subsequent requests.
warnings: Never store plain-text passwords — always hash with bcrypt or similar
JWT tokens are signed but not encrypted — do not store sensitive data in the payload
Always use HTTPS in production to protect tokens in transit

codeBlocks.label: Install dependencies
codeBlocks.code: pip install python-jose[cryptography] passlib[bcrypt] python-multipart

topics.title: OAuth2
topics.description: FastAPI's OAuth2PasswordBearer is a dependency that extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header. It is used alongside a login endpoint that accepts username and password (as form data) and returns a token. The tokenUrl parameter points to your login endpoint and is shown in the Swagger UI.
warnings: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm reads form data, not JSON — the login endpoint must accept form data
The token returned from the login endpoint should include token_type: 'bearer'

codeBlocks.label: OAuth2 setup
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm

app = FastAPI()
oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/token")

@app.post("/token")
async def login(form_data: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm = Depends()):
    # Validate username and password (see password-hashing)
    access_token = create_access_token({"sub": form_data.username})
    return {"access_token": access_token, "token_type": "bearer"}

@app.get("/me")
async def get_me(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    user = decode_token(token)  # see jwt-tokens
    return user

apiReference.name: OAuth2PasswordBearer
apiReference.description: Security dependency that extracts a Bearer token from the Authorization header.

params.name: tokenUrl
params.desc: URL of the login endpoint that issues tokens

params.name: auto_error
params.desc: If True, raises 401 automatically when token is missing

apiReference.returns: The token string from the Authorization header

topics.title: JWT Tokens
topics.description: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are a compact, URL-safe way to transmit signed claims between parties. FastAPI uses the python-jose library to create and verify JWTs. A secret key signs the token; any tampering invalidates the signature. Tokens typically have an expiry time (exp claim) to limit their validity window.
warnings: Use a strong, random SECRET_KEY — never hardcode it; load from environment variables
Set a short token expiry (e.g. 15-30 minutes) for access tokens

codeBlocks.label: JWT create and decode

topics.title: Password Hashing
topics.description: Passwords must never be stored in plain text. The passlib library with bcrypt backend is the standard choice for FastAPI applications. The CryptContext class provides a high-level interface for hashing and verifying passwords. Use verify() to check a plain password against its stored hash.
warnings: Never compare passwords with ==  — always use the verify() method
The bcrypt hash includes the salt, so each call to hash() produces a different result — this is expected

codeBlocks.label: Password hashing

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: Middleware in FastAPI runs on every request and response, before route handlers execute and after they return. It is ideal for cross-cutting concerns like logging, request timing, authentication, and adding response headers. FastAPI uses Starlette's middleware system and supports both function-based and class-based middleware.
warnings: Heavy middleware can slow down every single request — keep middleware logic fast
Middleware runs before Depends() — you cannot inject route-level dependencies in middleware

codeBlocks.label: Basic middleware
codeBlocks.code:
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    duration = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(duration)
    return response

topics.title: Custom Middleware
topics.description: Custom middleware is added using the @app.middleware('http') decorator or by subclassing BaseHTTPMiddleware. The middleware receives the Request and a call_next callable. You can inspect/modify the request before calling call_next, and inspect/modify the response afterwards. Class-based middleware using add_middleware() is the preferred pattern for reusable middleware.
warnings: Always await call_next(request) and return its response — forgetting this will hang the request
Do not read request.body() in middleware if your route also reads it — it can only be consumed once

codeBlocks.label: Class-based middleware
codeBlocks.code:
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

class LoggingMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        print(f"{request.method} {request.url.path}")
        response = await call_next(request)
        print(f"Status: {response.status_code}")
        return response

app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(LoggingMiddleware)

topics.title: CORS
topics.description: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is a browser security mechanism that blocks frontend JavaScript from making requests to a different domain than the one that served the page. FastAPI includes CORSMiddleware (from Starlette) to configure which origins are allowed. In development, allow_origins=['*'] is convenient but should never be used in production.
warnings: Never use allow_origins=['*'] with allow_credentials=True — this is a security vulnerability
In production, specify exact allowed origins (e.g., your frontend domain)

codeBlocks.label: CORS setup
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["http://localhost:3000", "https://myapp.com"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"],
    allow_headers=["Authorization", "Content-Type"],
)

apiReference.name: CORSMiddleware
apiReference.signature: CORSMiddleware(app, allow_origins, allow_credentials, allow_methods, allow_headers, max_age)
apiReference.description: Middleware for handling Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) preflight and actual requests.

params.name: allow_origins
params.desc: List of allowed origin URLs. Use ['*'] to allow all (not recommended in production).

params.name: allow_credentials
params.desc: Allow cookies and Authorization headers with cross-origin requests

params.name: allow_methods
params.desc: HTTP methods allowed for cross-origin requests

params.name: allow_headers
params.desc: HTTP headers allowed in cross-origin requests

topics.title: Trusted Hosts
topics.description: TrustedHostMiddleware is a security middleware that validates the Host header of incoming requests against a list of allowed hostnames. It protects against HTTP Host header attacks. Any request with a non-matching Host header receives a 400 Bad Request response.
warnings: Always configure TrustedHostMiddleware in production to prevent Host header injection attacks
Include both www and non-www variants of your domain in allowed_hosts

codeBlocks.label: TrustedHostMiddleware
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.middleware.trustedhost import TrustedHostMiddleware

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(
    TrustedHostMiddleware,
    allowed_hosts=["myapp.com", "www.myapp.com", "localhost"]
)

topics.title: Overview
topics.description: Background tasks in FastAPI allow you to run functions after returning a response to the client, without blocking the response. This is ideal for sending emails, processing uploads, logging to external services, or any slow operation that doesn't need to complete before the client receives a response. FastAPI uses Starlette's BackgroundTasks for this purpose.
warnings: Background tasks run in the same process — they are not suitable for CPU-intensive work
For heavy background processing, use a dedicated task queue like Celery or ARQ

codeBlocks.label: Basic background task
codeBlocks.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks
import time

app = FastAPI()

def send_email(email: str, message: str):
    # Simulate sending email
    time.sleep(2)
    print(f"Email sent to {email}: {message}")

@app.post("/notify")
async def notify_user(
    email: str,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks
):
    background_tasks.add_task(send_email, email, "Welcome!")
    return {"message": "Notification queued"}  # returns immediately

topics.title: BackgroundTasks
topics.description: The BackgroundTasks class is injected as a route parameter and accumulates tasks via add_task(). You can add multiple tasks in a single request. Tasks run after the response is sent. They can also be added inside dependencies, which is useful for audit logging and cleanup operations.
warnings: Background tasks share the event loop with the main app — avoid blocking sync code; use asyncio.to_thread() for blocking operations
Tasks are not persisted — if the server crashes, queued tasks are lost

codeBlocks.label: Multiple background tasks

apiReference.name: BackgroundTasks.add_task()
apiReference.description: Schedules a function to be called after the response is sent.

params.name: func
params.desc: The function to call in the background

params.name: *args
params.desc: Positional arguments passed to the function

params.name: **kwargs
params.desc: Keyword arguments passed to the function

topics.title: Lifespan Events
topics.description: Lifespan events allow you to run code at application startup and shutdown using a single async context manager. This replaced the deprecated @app.on_event() pattern. Common use cases are initializing database connection pools, loading ML models into memory, and closing connections cleanly on shutdown.
warnings: The lifespan pattern replaces the deprecated @app.on_event('startup') and @app.on_event('shutdown')
Startup errors will prevent the application from starting — handle exceptions carefully

codeBlocks.label: Lifespan context manager
codeBlocks.code:
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI

ml_models = {}

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup: runs before accepting requests
    print("Loading ML model...")
    ml_models["classifier"] = load_model("model.pkl")
    
    yield  # Application runs here
    
    # Shutdown: runs when app is stopping
    print("Cleaning up...")
    ml_models.clear()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

@app.get("/predict")
def predict(text: str):
    model = ml_models["classifier"]
    return {"prediction": model.predict(text)}
